sections.heading: What is Key-Value Storage in Android?
sections.content: Android provides lightweight key-value storage mechanisms for persisting small amounts of data — user preferences, session tokens, app settings, onboarding completion flags, and similar simple values. There are two primary options: the older SharedPreferences API and the modern Jetpack DataStore library. Both store data as key-value pairs, but DataStore is built on Kotlin coroutines and Flow, making it asynchronous, safe, and far more aligned with modern Android architecture. Google officially recommends DataStore over SharedPreferences for all new development.

sections.heading: When to Use Key-Value Storage
items: User preferences — dark mode toggle, notification settings, language selection
Session data — auth tokens, user ID of the logged-in user
App state flags — is_onboarding_complete, has_rated_app, first_launch_date
UI state — last selected tab, last scroll position, last search query
Simple configuration — base URL for debug builds, feature flags
Do NOT use for complex relational data — use Room Database instead
Do NOT use for large binary data — use file storage or Room with blob columns
Do NOT use for sensitive data like passwords — use Android Keystore or EncryptedSharedPreferences

sections.heading: SharedPreferences vs Preferences DataStore vs Proto DataStore

rows.preferences_datastore: Asynchronous — built on Kotlin coroutines and Flow
rows.proto_datastore: Asynchronous — uses Protocol Buffers for typed schema

rows.shared_preferences: Partially — apply() is async but get() is synchronous; can cause ANR on main thread
rows.preferences_datastore: Fully thread-safe — all operations are non-blocking coroutine-based

rows.shared_preferences: No — keys are strings; wrong type crashes at runtime
rows.proto_datastore: Full — schema defined in .proto file; compile-time type checking

rows.shared_preferences: No built-in error handling — silently fails or returns defaults
rows.preferences_datastore: Exceptions thrown as Flow errors — catchable with catch operator

rows.shared_preferences: Risk of data corruption under concurrent writes
rows.preferences_datastore: Transactional — uses DataStore's atomic write mechanism

rows.proto_datastore: High — requires .proto schema definition and code generation
Recommended when typed schema and complex objects are needed

sections.heading: SharedPreferences — Overview
sections.content: SharedPreferences is Android's original key-value storage API. It stores data in an XML file on the device's internal storage. While simple to use, SharedPreferences has significant drawbacks: its read and write operations can block the main thread causing ANR (Application Not Responding) errors, it has no built-in error handling, it is not safe for concurrent access, and it does not integrate with Kotlin's type system or coroutines. Despite these issues, understanding SharedPreferences is important because it is still widely used in existing codebases.

sections.heading: SharedPreferences — Basic Usage
items: Get instance: val prefs = context.getSharedPreferences("my_prefs", Context.MODE_PRIVATE)
Read a value: val isDarkMode = prefs.getBoolean("dark_mode", false)
Read a String: val token = prefs.getString("auth_token", null)
Write values using an Editor: prefs.edit() { putBoolean("dark_mode", true) }
apply() — writes asynchronously to disk in the background (preferred over commit())
commit() — writes synchronously and returns a Boolean success result (avoid on main thread)
Remove a key: prefs.edit() { remove("auth_token") }
Clear all keys: prefs.edit() { clear() }
Listen for changes: prefs.registerOnSharedPreferenceChangeListener(listener)

sections.heading: Jetpack DataStore — The Modern Replacement
sections.content: Jetpack DataStore is Google's modern replacement for SharedPreferences. It is built entirely on Kotlin coroutines and Flow, making it fully asynchronous and safe to use on any thread — including the main thread — without risking ANR errors. DataStore comes in two flavors: Preferences DataStore (key-value, no schema) and Proto DataStore (strongly typed with Protocol Buffers). For most use cases, Preferences DataStore is the right choice. It stores data in a file on disk, accessed safely through a Flow that emits the entire preferences snapshot whenever any value changes.

sections.heading: Setting Up Preferences DataStore
items: Add dependency: implementation("androidx.datastore:datastore-preferences:1.1.x")
Create a DataStore instance at the top level (outside any class): val Context.dataStore: DataStore<Preferences> by preferencesDataStore(name = "settings")
The preferencesDataStore delegate creates a singleton DataStore tied to the application context
Define keys using typed Preferences.Key objects: val DARK_MODE_KEY = booleanPreferencesKey("dark_mode")
Other key types: intPreferencesKey, longPreferencesKey, floatPreferencesKey, stringPreferencesKey, stringSetPreferencesKey
Read data as a Flow: context.dataStore.data.map { prefs -> prefs[DARK_MODE_KEY] ?: false }
Write data using edit(): context.dataStore.edit { prefs -> prefs[DARK_MODE_KEY] = true }
edit() is a suspend function — must be called from a coroutine scope

sections.heading: Reading Data from DataStore
items: DataStore exposes data as Flow<Preferences> — the entire preferences object emitted on every change
Use map operator to extract a specific value: dataStore.data.map { prefs -> prefs[DARK_MODE_KEY] ?: false }
The resulting Flow<Boolean> can be collected in a ViewModel or converted to StateFlow
Handle errors with the catch operator: .catch { e -> if (e is IOException) emit(emptyPreferences()) else throw e }
In the ViewModel: val isDarkMode = repo.darkModeFlow.stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), false)
stateIn() converts a cold Flow into a hot StateFlow with an initial value — safe to collect in the UI
The Flow automatically emits the latest value to new collectors immediately upon subscription
Each time any preference changes, the entire Preferences object is re-emitted — use map to extract only what you need

rows.notes: edit() is atomic and transactional — all changes in the block are written together
All changes in one edit() block are written atomically — no partial writes
Key is removed from the preferences file entirely
Removes all key-value pairs — use for logout or factory reset scenarios
Read current value inside edit() block for conditional writes

sections.heading: Wrapping DataStore in a Repository
sections.content: Following the Repository pattern, DataStore should not be accessed directly from a ViewModel. Instead, create a dedicated repository class — for example, UserPreferencesRepository — that wraps the DataStore instance and exposes clean, typed Flow properties and suspend functions. This keeps the ViewModel unaware of the DataStore implementation details and makes the preferences layer easy to test by injecting a fake repository.

sections.heading: UserPreferencesRepository Pattern
items: Create a class: class UserPreferencesRepository @Inject constructor(private val dataStore: DataStore<Preferences>)
Define keys as companion object constants inside the repository class
Expose typed Flow properties: val darkModeFlow: Flow<Boolean> = dataStore.data.map { it[DARK_MODE_KEY] ?: false }
Expose suspend write functions: suspend fun setDarkMode(enabled: Boolean) { dataStore.edit { it[DARK_MODE_KEY] = enabled } }
Inject DataStore in a Hilt module: @Provides @Singleton fun provideDataStore(@ApplicationContext context: Context) = context.dataStore
Inject the repository into the ViewModel: @HiltViewModel class SettingsViewModel @Inject constructor(private val repo: UserPreferencesRepository)
In the ViewModel: val isDarkMode = repo.darkModeFlow.stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), false)
Handle user action: fun setDarkMode(enabled: Boolean) { viewModelScope.launch { repo.setDarkMode(enabled) } }

sections.heading: DataStore Integration with ViewModel and UI

rows.description: val Context.dataStore by preferencesDataStore(name = "user_prefs") — top-level extension
UserPreferencesRepository wraps DataStore; exposes Flow<Boolean> darkModeFlow and suspend fun setDarkMode()
@Provides @Singleton fun provideDataStore(@ApplicationContext ctx: Context): DataStore<Preferences> = ctx.dataStore
@HiltViewModel class SettingsViewModel @Inject constructor(private val prefs: UserPreferencesRepository)

sections.heading: Proto DataStore — Typed Schema Storage
items: Proto DataStore uses Protocol Buffers (protobuf) to define a strict typed schema for stored data
Add dependencies: datastore and protobuf-javalite; configure the protobuf Gradle plugin
Define a .proto schema file in src/main/proto/ with message definitions for your data
Proto DataStore generates Kotlin classes from the .proto file — access fields with full type safety
Create a Serializer class implementing Serializer<YourProtoType> to handle reading and writing
Create with: val Context.settingsDataStore by dataStore(fileName = "settings.pb", serializer = SettingsSerializer)
Read: context.settingsDataStore.data.map { settings -> settings.darkMode }
Write: context.settingsDataStore.updateData { current -> current.toBuilder().setDarkMode(true).build() }

rows.preferences_datastore: No schema — keys defined in code with Preferences.Key
rows.proto_datastore: Strict schema defined in a .proto file

rows.preferences_datastore: Partial — key type is enforced but schema can drift
rows.proto_datastore: Full — schema is the contract; compile-time enforcement

rows.preferences_datastore: Low — one dependency, no schema file
rows.proto_datastore: High — requires protobuf plugin, .proto file, and Serializer class

rows.preferences_datastore: Not supported — only primitives and String sets
rows.proto_datastore: Supported — nested messages, lists, and enums
Manual — must define schema and migration manually
Complex user profile data, typed configuration objects

sections.heading: Migrating from SharedPreferences to DataStore
items: DataStore provides SharedPreferencesMigration to automatically migrate existing SharedPreferences data
Pass migration in the preferencesDataStore delegate: preferencesDataStore(name = "settings", produceMigrations = { context -> listOf(SharedPreferencesMigration(context, "old_prefs_name")) })
Migration runs automatically the first time DataStore is accessed — SharedPreferences data is copied over
After migration, SharedPreferences file is deleted to avoid duplicate state
Keys are migrated by name — SharedPreferences key "dark_mode" maps to a DataStore key named "dark_mode"
Test migration thoroughly — especially for keys with type differences between the two systems
Migration is a one-time operation — once complete, always read and write via DataStore going forward
Keep the old SharedPreferences name in the migration call even after the file is deleted — the migration code handles this

rows.mistake: Creating multiple DataStore instances with the same file name
rows.fix: Use a singleton DataStore via the preferencesDataStore delegate at the top level — one instance per file name

rows.mistake: Calling edit() from the main thread without a coroutine
rows.fix: edit() is a suspend function — always call it inside viewModelScope.launch or another coroutine scope

rows.mistake: Catching IOException in the ViewModel instead of the Repository
rows.fix: Handle IOException in the Repository's Flow with catch { e -> if (e is IOException) emit(emptyPreferences()) }

rows.mistake: Using DataStore for large or complex objects
rows.fix: DataStore is for simple key-value preferences — use Room for complex relational data

rows.mistake: Accessing DataStore.data directly in the UI
rows.fix: Always wrap DataStore in a Repository and expose typed Flow properties — never access DataStore directly from Fragment

rows.mistake: Not providing an initial value to stateIn()
rows.fix: Always provide a sensible default initial value in stateIn() so the UI has something to show before the first emission

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: SharedPreferences served Android developers well for over a decade, but its synchronous API, lack of error handling, and thread safety issues make it unsuitable for modern Android development. Jetpack DataStore replaces it with a fully asynchronous, coroutine-based, Flow-driven alternative that integrates naturally with MVVM architecture. Wrap DataStore in a Repository, expose preferences as typed Flow properties, convert to StateFlow in the ViewModel, and collect safely in the UI — this pattern gives you a clean, testable, and reactive preferences layer for any Android app.
